g.text: Substitute User | Switch User | Privilege Elevation

g.data-narration: The su command lets you switch to another user account without logging out. It's essential for system administration.

text.tspan: A Linux command that allows switching to another user account without
logging out, providing temporary access to that user's privileges and environment.

g.data-narration: The su command provides quick privilege elevation. Without logging out, you can become root or any other user.

text.tspan: Switch to root or any user

g.text: Defaults to root if no username

g.data-narration: The hyphen option starts a login shell with proper environment setup. This is the recommended way to use su.
Compare su and su minus: one preserves environment, the other creates a fresh login shell with correct PATH and home directory.
The root user has complete system control. When using su to become root, the prompt changes from dollar to hash symbol.

text.tspan: Root user has complete control over the system (UID 0).

g.data-narration: Example 1 shows switching to root interactively. Type su -, enter password, and get root privileges with the hash prompt.
The -c option lets you run one command as another user without starting an interactive shell. Control returns immediately.

text.tspan: Executes one command as target user

g.data-narration: You can switch to any user account. This requires the target user's password. Useful for troubleshooting or accessing files.
Scripts commonly use su to run operations with specific user permissions, like running backup scripts as appuser.
su requires target user's password while sudo uses your own. Sudo provides better security and audit trails.
Environment variables behave differently with su options. Without dash, old PATH persists; with dash, user's profile loads properly.
PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) controls su access. The /etc/pam.d/su file configures who can use su and how.
Always use the exit command to return to your original user account. Never leave sessions running as root.
g.text: Always exit after using su to avoid

g.data-narration: Remember these key exam points: su defaults to root, hyphen creates login shell, requires target password, prompt changes to hash.
g.text: Exam Tips - Key Points to Remember
su without username defaults to root
su - creates login shell with proper environment
Use exit to return to original user

g.data-narration: Avoid common mistakes: forgetting the dash causes environment issues, not exiting leaves you as wrong user.
The su command is fundamental for Linux privilege elevation. Master these concepts for exams and system administration.
Practice su and su - to understand environment differences. Use whoami to verify your current user identity.
